title: Dog Depressed After Boarding: Normal Behavior or Cause for Concern?
meta_desc: Is your dog acting lethargic or sad after boarding? Learn why dogs get post-boarding blues and when you should contact your veterinarian for help.
content_html:
What Are Post-Boarding Blues?

It is very common for dogs to exhibit a change in mood or energy after returning home from a boarding facility. This 'depressed' attitude is usually a combination of physical exhaustion from a high-stimulation environment and a period of emotional decompression as they transition back to their normal routine.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. While it can be heart-wrenching to see your pet acting less like themselves, a quiet or sleepy demeanor after boarding is rarely an emergency. Most dogs simply need a few days of rest and familiar surroundings to bounce back. However, keep a close eye on their appetite and bathroom habits; if they stop eating entirely or show signs of physical distress, a check-up is warranted.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a quick photo or video of your dog's behavior can be an invaluable tool for your veterinarian. Visual evidence of their posture, facial expressions, and overall movement allows a professional to more accurately assess whether your dog is simply tired or if they are displaying signs of pain or illness that may have been masked by the stress of the kennel.

faqs.question: How long will my dog act sad after boarding?
faqs.answer: Most dogs return to their normal selves within two to five days as they catch up on sleep and settle back into their home routine.

faqs.question: Is it normal for my dog to sleep more after a kennel stay?
faqs.answer: Yes, boarding facilities are often loud and highly stimulating, meaning many dogs do not get quality sleep while away. They often need several days of deep rest once they return home.

faqs.question: When should I call the vet about post-boarding lethargy?
faqs.answer: You should call the vet if the lethargy is accompanied by coughing, persistent diarrhea, vomiting, or a total refusal to eat for more than 24 hours.
